windva's blog

Codeforces Round 899 (Div. 2) Editorial
By windva, history, 3 years ago,

1882A - Increasing Sequence

Tutorial

Greedy solution. Continue constructing bb as small as possible.

If a1=1a1=1, b1=2b1=2. Else, b1=1b1=1.

For i≥2i≥2, if ai=bi−1+1ai=bi−1+1, bi=bi−1+2bi=bi−1+2. Else, bi=bi−1+1bi=bi−1+1.

bnbn calculated by this process is the answer.

Time complexity is O(n)O(n) per test case.

1882B - Sets and Union

Something to say

Sorry for everyone who got FSTs :( We tried our best to make pretest strong especially for this problem, but it wasn't enough.

Hint 1

Denote T=S1∪S2∪⋯∪SnT=S1∪S2∪⋯∪Sn, then S⊂TS⊂T. Since S≠TS≠T, i∈Ti∈T and i∉Si∉S for some ii.

Hint 2

Given an integer ii, can you calculate the maximum size of SS, such that i∉Si∉S?

Tutorial

i∈Ti∈T and i∉Si∉S for some ii. Here 1≤i≤501≤i≤50.

For fixed ii, select of all the sets among S1,S2,⋯,SnS1,S2,⋯,Sn which don't contain ii. Size of their union will be the maximum size of SS such that i∉Si∉S.

If we do this for all ii in TT, maximum of them is the answer.

Time complexity is O(N⋅max(si,j)2)O(N⋅max(si,j)2).

1882C - Card Game

Hint 1

Fix the topmost card you'll pick in the initial deck.

Tutorial

Let's denote ii-th card in the initial deck as card ii.

Let the topmost card you'll pick in the initial deck as card ii.

For all cards under card ii in initial deck, you can choose all and only cards with the positive value at odd index.

Proof: Here is the strategy. Before you pick card ii, if positive card(under card ii in initial deck) in odd index exists, choose it. Repeat this until all positive cards(under card ii in initial deck) are in even index. Then if you choose card ii, all index of positive cards(under card ii in initial deck) will be decreased by 11, and will become odd. Now, choose them from the bottom to top, so that the choosing won't change the other positive cards' index.

Denote prfjprfj as the sum of positive numbers among aj,aj+1,⋯,anaj,aj+1,⋯,an, and prfn+1=0prfn+1=0. Since prfj=prfj+1+max(0,aj)prfj=prfj+1+max(0,aj), prfprf can be calculated in O(n)O(n).

You should necessarily pick card ii in index of ii, and can pick all positive cards under card ii in initial deck, so your maximum final score will be (i%2==1?ai:0)+prfi+1(i%2==1?ai:0)+prfi+1.

The answer is max((i%2==1?ai:0)+prfi+1|1≤i≤n)max((i%2==1?ai:0)+prfi+1|1≤i≤n).

There are lots of other solutions too.

1882D - Tree XOR

Hint 1

Try to solve the problem for single root.

Hint 2

For any vertex vv which isn't the root, denote parvparv as the parent of vv. Then value of av⊕aparvav⊕aparv only changes if we perform the operation on vv.

Hint 3

Since x1⊕x2⊕⋯⊕xm≤x1+x2+⋯+xmx1⊕x2⊕⋯⊕xm≤x1+x2+⋯+xm for any numbers x1,x2,⋯,xmx1,x2,⋯,xm, we can assume that there is at most operation performed in same vertex.

Tutorial

Let's solve the problem for single root first.

Denote the operation in the statement as op(v,c)op(v,c), and size of vv's subtree as svsv.

The goal is to make av⊕aparv=0av⊕aparv=0 for every non-root vertex vv. This value is changed only when we select vv. To make av⊕aparvav⊕aparv to 00, we should perform op(v,av⊕aparv)op(v,av⊕aparv) (Look Hint 3).

Therefore, the answer sill be ∑i!=rootsi×(ai⊕apari)∑i!=rootsi×(ai⊕apari). Now our task is to calculate this for all roots. This can be done by lots of ways, and the following algorithm is one way.

Calculate the answer for 11 as root first in O(n)O(n). Now, we will traverse the tree starting at vertex 11 and keep updating the answer. If root changes from qq to rr (qq and rr are adjacent), every vertex except qq and rr will have same parents and subtree size, so will contribute same to the answer, so we only need to consider qq and rr to calculate the change. Edge connecting qq and rr will divide the tree into parts: each of size XX and YY. If root changes qq to rr, Y×(aq⊕ar)Y×(aq⊕ar) will be subtracted, and X×(aq⊕ar)X×(aq⊕ar) will be added to the answer. XX and YY can be pre-calculated in O(n)O(n), so this update costs O(1)O(1).

Since changing root into the adjacent vertex costs O(1)O(1), answer for all roots can be calculated in O(n)O(n).

1882E1 - Two Permutations (Easy Version)

Hint 1

Let's think two permutations independently. The goal is to make sequence of operations for each permutation which have same parity of number of operations.

Hint 2

Try to sort single permutation of length NN, using at most 3N3N operations. It is always possible.

Hint 3

If the permutation's length is odd, you can perform operation at index 11 for NN times and return to the same permutation.

Hint 4

If the permutation's length is even, the parity of inversion number changes in each operations.

Tutorial

Hints above were the summary of the tutorial. Please check them.

First, let's do Hint 2. There are lots of ways to do this, and I'd like to introduce one which I thought first. It is possible to swap two elements using 33 operations. Let's denote the two elements as xx and yy, and permutation as [[A]x[B]y[C]][[A]x[B]y[C]] ([A],[B],[C][A],[B],[C] are subarrays). Then:

  1. Perform the operation at xx. Permutation becomes [[B]y[C]x[A]][[B]y[C]x[A]].
  2. Perform the operation at yy. Permutation becomes [[C]x[A]y[B]][[C]x[A]y[B]].
  3. Perform the operation at xx. Permutation becomes [[A]y[B]x[C]][[A]y[B]x[C]].

Using this, we can sort the single permutation of length NN using at most 3N3N operations, since we can sort the permutation by NN swaps.

If this requires same parity of number of operations for pp and qq, the problem is solved. At most 3max(m,n)3max(m,n) operations are used.

Else, if nn or mm is odd, we can make the parity equal by method provided in Hint 3. At most 4max(m,n)4max(m,n) operations are used.

Else, then nn and mm is both even. In this case, it's impossible because of Hint 4.

The overall time complexity is O(n+m)O(n+m) in this solution.

Lastly, here is the proof of Hint 4.

Proof: Let's consider the permutation of length NN(NN is even). Denote the permutation as [[A]x[B]][[A]x[B]], and the length of [A][A] and [B][B] as nAnA and nBnB. Here nA+nB=N−1nA+nB=N−1 so one of nAnA and nBnB is even and one of them is odd. Permutation becomes [[B]x[A]][[B]x[A]] after the operation.

First, the parity of inversion number from two elements of [A][A] or two elements of [B][B] doesn't change, because their order inside doesn't change.

Second, the parity of inversion number from one element of [A][A] and one element of [B][B] doesn't change, because sum of them are nA×nBnA×nB, which is even.

Third, the parity of inversion number from xx and one element of [A][A] or [B][B] changes, because sum of them are nA+nBnA+nB, which is odd.

If we add these three, we can lead to the conclusion that the parity of inversion number must change. The text may look a bit complicated, but it will not be that hard if you write them in your own :)

1882E2 - Two Permutations (Hard Version)

Special thanks to kizen providing an key idea which motivated me to make this problem!

Hint 1

The solution is completely different from E1. A brilliant idea is required. Maybe the fact that checker is implemented in linear time might be the hint.

Hint 2

Find a way changing the operation to 'swap'.

Hint 3

Add an extra character.

Tutorial

Add an extra character 'XX' in front of the permutation. Here position of XX won't be changed by the operation, and will always locate at left of p1p1. Then in each operation, the permutation will change as: [X[A]c[B]]→[X[B]c[A]][X[A]c[B]]→[X[B]c[A]].

Now, let's consider the array made by XX and permutation as circular. This is possible because XX is always in left of 11st element, so it marks the start of the permutation. Then [X[B]c[A]][X[B]c[A]] is equivalent with [c[A]X[B]][c[A]X[B]].

Then the operation is: [X[A]c[B]]→[c[A]X[B]][X[A]c[B]]→[c[A]X[B]], which is same with swapping XX and cc.

Now we need to calculate the minimum odd number of swaps and even number of swaps(of XX and any element) each, turning [Xp1p2⋯pn][Xp1p2⋯pn] to one of [X12⋯n][X12⋯n], [nX12⋯(n−1)][nX12⋯(n−1)], [(n−1)nX1⋯(n−2)][(n−1)nX1⋯(n−2)], ⋯⋯, [12⋯nX][12⋯nX].

To calculate the minimum number of swaps required to turn [Xp1p2⋯pn][Xp1p2⋯pn] to the given array, first renumber the initial array to [X12⋯n][X12⋯n], then change the given array in the same correspondence. Do permutation cycle decomposition. Then the answer is (sum of (size + 1) for cycles which have size ≥≥ 2 and don't contain XX) + (XX's cycle size −− 1). This can be proven easily by counting the number of elements which go into the proper place in each operations.

Calculate this for all [X12⋯n][X12⋯n], [nX12⋯(n−1)][nX12⋯(n−1)], [(n−1)nX1⋯(n−2)][(n−1)nX1⋯(n−2)], ⋯⋯, [12⋯nX][12⋯nX]. Since we can't make the same array using different parity of number of swaps, we can achieve the goal by calculating the minimum odd number and minimum even number each.

The overall time complexity is O(n2+m2)O(n2+m2).

Challenge

Try solving E1 in n,m≤105n,m≤105, using at most 1.5×1051.5×105 operations. It is solvable :)

Tutorial of Codeforces Round 899 (Div. 2)

Can anybody explain me the DP solution for C in an easy way?!

→ Reply
  • »

    drmr
    3 years ago, show #
    »
    »
    drmr
    3 years ago, hide # ^ |
    ← Rev. 3 →  
    0 
    #include <bits/stdc++.h>
#define int long long
using namespace std;
int n, a[300000], b[300000], ans, T;
void init(){
	cin >> n;
	for(int i = 1; i <= n; i++) cin >> a[i];
	b[n + 1] = 0;
	for(int i = n; i >= 1; i--){
		b[i] = b[i + 1] + max(a[i], 0ll);
	}
	ans = 0;
	for(int i = 1; i <= n; i++){
		if(i & 1) ans = max(ans, b[i + 1] + a[i]);
		else ans = max(ans, b[i + 1]);
	}
	cout << ans << endl;
	return ;
}
signed main(){
	ios::sync_with_stdio(false);
	cin.tie(0); cout.tie(0);
	cin >> T;
	while(T--){
		init();
	}
	return 0;
}
    → Reply

    talibsid
    3 years ago, show (+3) #
    »
    »
    talibsid
    3 years ago, hide # ^ |
     
    +5 
    // Intial DP States for the last index
dp[n][1] = max(a[n], 0LL);
dp[n][0] = 0LL;

// To avoid negative takes, we max it with 0
ll ans = max(0LL, dp[n][n & 1]);

// At every index, we will be computing optimal values for both odd and even cases
for (int i = n - 1; i >= 1; i--) {
	// Optimal Value for even index
    dp[i][0] = max(dp[i+1][0], dp[i+1][1]);
	// Optimal Value for odd index
    dp[i][1] = max(dp[i+1][0] + max(0LL, a[i]), dp[i+1][1] + a[i]);
    
    // dp [i][i&1] is the optimal value that can be taken 
    // for that index, with its current parity
    ans = max(ans, dp[i][i & 1]);
}
cout << ans << '\n';

    credits: benjamin_kleyn

      why dp[n][1] and dp[n][0] are like that ? what dp[i][j] in general means and how do you got the intuition behind considering 2D dp ? can it be reduced to 1D dp also ?benjamin_kleyn

        benjamin_kleyn
        3 years ago, hide # ^ |
         
        0

        dp[i][0]dp[i][0] is the answer if we only consider the suffix ai,ai+1,…anai,ai+1,…an, and we consider ii to be an "even index".

        dp[i][1]dp[i][1] is the answer if we only consider the suffix ai,ai+1,…anai,ai+1,…an, and we consider ii to be an "odd index".

        Thus we print the maximal value of dp[i][i%2]dp[i][i%2] over all ii.

    chromate00
    3 years ago, show #
    »
    »
    chromate00
    3 years ago, hide # ^ |
     
    0

    Let us assume we have two certain cards aa and bb, where WLOG aa comes before bb. If we use bb before aa, the parity of the indices are preserved. However, if we use aa before bb, the parity of the index on bb changes. The key insight is that, generalizing this to more than two cards, we can choose any card's parity of index as long as we want to. (Of course, the first card chosen is an exception, but we'll get to that in a sec)

    Now define dp[i][change parity?]dp[i][change parity?] as the maximum value of the three cases —

    • You choose the ii-th card, while changing its parity of index.
    • You choose the ii-th card, without changing its parity of index.
    • You do not choose the ii-th card.

    Now, if we define dp[0][0]=0dp[0][0]=0 and dp[0][1]=−∞dp[0][1]=−∞, the case of changing the first card's parity is automagically cleared out. This is because when you try to change the first card's parity, it is evaluated as −∞+c=−∞−∞+c=−∞.

    Here you can see the code based on the approach. 225166689

    → Reply

← Rev. 4 →  
+1

Recursive DP solution for Problem C


int recur(int i, int parity, vector<int> &a, vector<vector<int>> &dp)
{
  if (i >= a.size())
    return 0;
  int &ans = dp[i][parity];
  if (ans != -1)
    return ans;
  ans = recur(i + 1, parity, a, dp);
  if ((i % 2) == parity)
  {
    ans = max(ans, a[i] + recur(i + 1, parity ^ 1, a, dp));
    ans = max(ans, a[i] + recur(i + 2, parity, a, dp));
  }
  else
    ans = max(ans, recur(i + 1, parity ^ 1, a, dp));

  return ans;
}
→ Reply

Can anyone tell me how E1?

I know how to sort the 22 arrays, but not understanding how to make the number of operations the same.

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    timreizin
    3 years ago, show #
    »
    »
    timreizin
    3 years ago, hide # ^ |
     
    +11

    If they have the same parity of the number of operations just do 1 and n on the smaller sequence of operations until its length reaches the bigger one. Otherwise they have different parity. If both permutations have even length answer is -1. Otherwise WLOG permutation a has odd length, then just do operation 1 for n times. Now parities are the same and we know how to solve that.

    → Reply
  • »
    »
    pacha2880
    3 years ago, show #
    »
    »
    pacha2880
    3 years ago, hide # ^ |
     
    +1

    If the difference of number of operations is even you can just type 1 and n or m the times you need depending on which one is less. If the difference is odd, you can make it even if n or m are odd, you just type 1 the size of the array times till it goes back to be sorted.

← Rev. 4 →  
+11

In Problem C, if ever you can only choose it from a_1 or a_2 the first value which is picked, there are optimal solutions. It is a funny property and here is a simple solution using this fact:

ll S = max(a[0], 0LL)
if(n > 1) S = max(S, a[0] + a[1]);
for(int i=2;i<n;i++) if(a[i] > 0) S += a[i];
cout << S << "\n";

Alternative Solution for C 1.First of all you only need to care about first two index.lets see how....there are only four possibility for these two index..either both pos ,both neg, first is pos second is neg, and first is neg second is pos...lets deal with all of them one by one but before that i am assuming that we have picked all the pos numbers which are at odd pos after 2nd index and there are only pos elements left at even index(after 2nd index)...now for the first case when both are neg we can always remove 2nd index neg and now all the even index pos after that comes at odd index so we can add the rest of pos....same goes for the second case when both are pos,you can pick first index and now all the rest pos....in the case of first pos and second neg...remove second index element(neg number) and now all the pos are at odd index....for the last case first is neg and second is pos,one thing is to notice that if you have to remove atleast one pos number to get the pos after it(because then they come at odd pos) now which pos to choose...obviously removing the 2nd index pos number is always favourable as now we can always add all the pos numbers after it...so what we observe that we will always take all the pos numbers after 2nd index and for these two elements we can check locally...see my submission 225272412.Thanks for reading upto here.

in E2 , I couldnt understand what editorial was trying to say , would greatly appreaciate it if you could explain it in a simpler manner

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    tinca_matei
    3 years ago, show (+3) #
    »
    »
    tinca_matei
    3 years ago, hide # ^ |
     
    +23

    I'm also trying to understand, but essentially, you add the XX at the beginning of the array. Now, your array will be XX p1p1 p2p2 ... pnpn. If you do an operation on an array XX AA bb CC, where b is the pivot and A and C are the two subarrays, the array becomes XX CC bb AA. At the moment, XX stays in place, because it's just an artificial element at the start of the array.

    Now, the main trick is that you consider this array as a circular array. Here, XX tells you the start of the array, so if you start reading the array from the X, you will get the original array. Because the array is circular, the array XX p1p1 p2p2 ... pnpn is equivalent to every other rotation of the array (i.e. [ pnpn XX p1p1 p2p2 ... pn−1pn−1]; [ pn−1pn−1 pnpn XX p1p1 p2p2 ... pn−2pn−2] ; ...; [ p1p1 p2p2 ... pnpn XX ] ).

    Therefore, XX CC bb AA is equivalent to bb AA XX CC. So, from XX AA bb CC, the operation transforms it into bb AA XX CC. Essentially, what this operation does is that it swaps XX with any other element.

    So, we will start with the array XX p1p1 p2p2 ... pnpn (just the initial array) and then perform swaps with XX and other elements so that we reach the sorted array, which is XX 11 22 ... nn. Since both the starting and ending arrays are so far considered circular arrays, we will stop doing so and do it on a simple array. As a consequence, the possible "target" arrays are [ XX 11 22 ... nn ] , [ nn XX 11 22 ... n−1n−1 ], [ n−1n−1 nn XX 11 22 ... n−2n−2 ] , ..., [ 11 22 ... nn XX ]. We will try to find a sequence of operations for every of the possible "target" arrays.

    So now we must solve the following problem: we have the array XX p1p1 p2p2 ... pnpn, with an operation, we swap element XX with some other element, and we want to reach some other permutation (in particular, one of the above "target" arrays). I will replace XX with 00, so that we have a 0-indexed permutation of length n+1n+1. So, given 00 p1p1 p2p2 ... pnpn, we want to transform it into another target permutation. To do so, you decompose this permutation into cycles (i.e. see where 00 has to go, the element on that position where to go, and so on). You will use 00 as some sort of buffer. You will swap 00 with the element that has to go in its position, and you will do that until 00's cycle is solved. This will take len−1len−1 moves (each move solves an element, except the last one, which will put 00 in its correct position and the element that has to go in 00's position, thus solving two elements at the same time). To solve the other cycles in the permutation, you swap 00 with some other element in the cycle that you want to solve ("break into the cycle"), and then you do the same thing as above. Swap 00 with the element that has to go in 00's position. For this cycle, you use 1+len1+len swaps (first swap breaks into the cycle, the next lenlen swaps will solve each element in the cycle). Hence, you get the formula from the editorial: (sum of (size + 1) for cycles which have size ≥ 2 and don't contain X) + (X's cycle size − 1). Keep in mind, elements that are already solved which will form cycles of length 1 will be ignored.

    So, you can find out the minimum number of swaps between XX p1p1 p2p2 ... pnpn to any other array. In particular, you want to find the minimum number of swaps from the beginning permutation to every rotation of XX 11 22 ... nn. So you have a new array which gives you for each target rotation the minimum number of operations. You find the minimum even number and the minimum odd number. Everything I said above was for only one permutation, but you have two permutations in the input, so you do everything for both permutations. Take the minimum from the answers with the same parity (i.e. minimum odd number of operations for the first permutation and minimum odd number of operations for the second permutation, and the same for evens) and this is your answer. If you combine two solutions, let's say that they have size nn and mm, the number of operations used is max(n,m)max(n,m). If you have a solution of size nn, you can extend it to have size n+2n+2 (swap X with some element and do that swap again, to do nothing in two operations). You use this to pad one of the arrays with useless operations so that the two solutions have the same size.

    TLDR:

    • you consider the starting array as a circular array and prepend it with some extra character XX that represents the start of the array;
    • an operations swaps XX with some other element;
    • you can stop considering the array as a circular array from this point, but you have an extra element;
    • you want to transform the starting array in another array that is a rotation of XX 11 22 33 ... nn;
    • to transform a starting permutation to a target permutation, you decompose it into cycles and swap the elements around to solve the permutation;
    • you find the minimum number of operations to transform the starting array to every rotation of the target array; take the minimum odd number and the minimum even number;
    • to combine the two permutation in the input, combine odds with odds and evens with evens; to have two sequences of swaps of same length, pad one with pairs of operations that do nothing;
    → Reply

      »
      »
      luoyanjin
      3 years ago, show (+1) #
      »
      »
      »
      luoyanjin
      3 years ago, hide # ^ |
      ← Rev. 4 →  
      0

      Could you tell me how to demonstrate that we can certainly get both even and odd answers.What if we can only get an minimum even answer but there're minimum ansers with both parities?

      → Reply
      • »
        »
        »
        »
        windva
        3 years ago, show #
        »
        »
        »
        »
        windva
        3 years ago, hide # ^ |
         
        +8

        In general, there can't be both even sequence of swaps and odd sequence of swaps that turns the permutation to same other permutation, since each swap changes the parity of inversion number (this is well-known).

        Therefore, if the minimum number of swaps required to turn [Xp1⋯pn][Xp1⋯pn] into the array required is even, then there can't be the odd number of swaps that turns [Xp1⋯pn][Xp1⋯pn] into the array.

        So if there is no minimum odd number, then there is no any odd answer.

        Furthermore, for reference, if nn is odd, then the parity of number of operations required changes everytime when we shift the goal array once. Specifically, parity of number of number of operations required to turn [Xp1p2p3p4p5][Xp1p2p3p4p5] into [X12345][X12345], [2345X1][2345X1], [45X123][45X123] are equal, and [12345X][12345X], [345X12][345X12], [5X1234][5X1234] are equal(and these are different). This is because one shift can be obtained by nn swaps, nn is odd, and each swap changes the parity of number of cycles, and the number of operations is equal with nn + (number of cycles) in mod 2.

        Similarly, if nn is even, all parity are same.

        → Reply

How to solve the challenge with E? Can anyone give some tips? Thanks

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    windva
    3 years ago, show #
    »
    »
    windva
    3 years ago, hide # ^ |
    ← Rev. 2 →  
    0

    The number of operations in a single permutation required is,

    (sum of (size + 1) for cycles without X and size >= 2) + (X's cycle size — 1).

    This value is at most 1.5n in a permutation of length n.

    However, if we do this with both permutations, and the parity of them are different, we should equal the parity. Surprisingly this can be done in only 1 operation (think why :))

    → Reply

    2.

    3.

    4. 

  4.  
    5. 

  5. def no_one_repeat(array1, array2):
    6. 

  6.     for a, b in zip(array1, array2):
    7. 

  7.         if a == b:
    8. 

  8.             return False
    9.

    10. 

  10.  
    11. 

  11.  
    12. 

  12. for x in range(n):

    24.

    25.

        tinca_matei
        3 years ago, hide # ^ |
         
        +10

        You're only trying arrays of type x,x+1,x+2,...,x+n−1x,x+1,x+2,...,x+n−1, so only a particular class of arrays. For n=5n=5, let's say the largest number is 88, so the sequence you're trying is [4,5,6,7,8][4,5,6,7,8]. Let's also say that 88 is the correct answer to the problem. if a1=4a1=4, then you will say that 88 is not actually the answer, so you must go higher. Basically, you're filtering this answer out because you're trying only one particular class of arrays.
